summary:
The Treaty of Karlowitz (January 1699 AD) was the landmark peace agreement that ended the [[event:3631|Great Turkish War]] and marked the decisive turning point in the long [[event:2324|Ottoman-Habsburg conflict]] — the first major Ottoman territorial concession in Europe and the beginning of the empire's gradual retreat from Central Europe. Signed at Sremski Karlovci (Karlowitz) after the Holy League's decisive victory at the Battle of Zenta (1697), the treaty transferred most of Hungary, Transylvania, Croatia, and Slavonia to the Habsburg Empire, awarded Podolia to Poland, and gave the Peloponnese and Dalmatia to Venice — collectively representing the Ottoman Empire's greatest territorial loss since its founding.

The treaty's strategic significance was profound. For over 150 years, the Ottoman Empire had been a major threat to Central European Christendom — the siege of Vienna in 1529 and again in 1683 had seemed to threaten the conquest of Christian Europe. Karlowitz ended this era: the Ottoman Empire never again seriously threatened Central Europe and would spend the next two centuries in defensive retreat. The treaty also established the Habsburg Empire as the dominant power in Central Europe and accelerated Ottoman internal reform debates. The Ottomans' loss of Hungary — the breadbasket of the empire and a source of enormous tribute revenue — was both an economic and psychological blow from which the empire's self-perception as an expanding world power never fully recovered.
